citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
A dog was identified at the facility needing veterinary care. The facility did not identify the issue prior to the inspection. An
adult female Bernese Mountain Dog (#74819) was found to have a wound on the inside tip of the right ear. The bleeding
wound measured approximately 0.5 inches with a dark crust and hair loss around the edges. Failure to conduct adequate
daily observations to assess the health and well-being of the animals with direct and frequent communication to the
attending veterinarian can delay treatment leading to the condition getting worse and delays in healing. Each dealer shall
establish and maintain programs of adequate veterinary care that include daily observation of all animals to assess their
health and well-being and that a mechanism of direct and frequent communication is required so that timely and accurate
information on problems of animal health, behavior, and well-being is conveyed to the attending veterinarian. Correct by
ensuring that the animal is examined and treated by a licensed veterinarian.

Enclosures are not being cleaned properly. The door leading from the inside to the outside runs of most of the enclosures
is soiled with a black/brown material. Inadequate and infrequent cleaning and sanitization allows for the growth of
disease-causing agents that may increase the risk of health hazards for the animals. Appropriate cleaning and sanitization
of the surfaces must include removal of the buildup of material found and to prevent accumulation of this material. All
surfaces of housing facilities must be cleaned and sanitized when necessary to satisfy generally accepted husbandry
standards and practices. Correct by ensuring hard surfaces with which the dogs come into contact with are spot cleaned
daily and sanitized in accordance with 3.11 (b) of this subpart to prevent accumulation of excreta and reduce disease
hazards.

Enclosures contain surfaces that are not impervious to moisture. In most of the outside runs, the wood trim above the
doors leading to the inside contains small chew marks revealing wood that are not impervious to moisture. Surfaces in
contact with the animals that are not impervious to moisture can absorb moisture and harbor bacteria while being unable
to be properly cleaned and sanitized possibly exposing the animals to disease hazards. Correct by ensuring all walls,
boxes, houses, dens, and other surfaces in contact with the animals in sheltered housing facilities are impervious to
moisture.
This is a 2nd Re-License Inspection for a Class “A” license. The inspection and exit briefing were conducted with the
applicant.
All items must be in compliance within 1 more inspection or by March 15, 2026 or the applicant will forfeit the license fee
and must wait 6 months to reapply. Please contact your inspector to schedule your next Re-License Inspection.
Regulated activity can continue under your existing USDA license until it expires.
n

narrative:
2.40(b)(3) 
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors). 
A dog was identified at the facility needing veterinary care. The facility did not identify the issue prior to the inspection. An 
adult female Bernese Mountain Dog (#74819) was found to have a wound on the inside tip of the right ear. The bleeding 
wound measured approximately 0.5 inches with a dark crust and hair loss around the edges. Failure to conduct adequate 
daily observations to assess the health and well-being of the animals with direct and frequent communication to the 
attending veterinarian can delay treatment leading to the condition getting worse and delays in healing. Each dealer shall 
establish and maintain programs of adequate veterinary care that include daily observation of all animals to assess their 
health and well-being and that a mechanism of direct and frequent communication is required so that timely and accurate 
information on problems of animal health, behavior, and well-being is conveyed to the attending veterinarian. Correct by 
ensuring that the animal is examined and treated by a licensed veterinarian. 
3.1(c)(3) 
   
Housing facilities, general. 
Enclosures are not being cleaned properly. The door leading from the inside to the outside runs of most of the enclosures 
is soiled with a black/brown material. Inadequate and infrequent cleaning and sanitization allows for the growth of 
disease-causing agents that may increase the risk of health hazards for the animals. Appropriate cleaning and sanitization 
of the surfaces must include removal of the buildup of material found and to prevent accumulation of this material. All 
surfaces of housing facilities must be cleaned and sanitized when necessary to satisfy generally accepted husbandry 
standards and practices. Correct by ensuring hard surfaces with which the dogs come into contact with are spot cleaned 
daily and sanitized in accordance with 3.11 (b) of this subpart to prevent accumulation of excreta and reduce disease 
hazards. 
3.3(e)(1)(iii) 
   
Sheltered housing facilities. 
Enclosures contain surfaces that are not impervious to moisture. In most of the outside runs, the wood trim above the 
doors leading to the inside contains small chew marks revealing wood that are not impervious to moisture. Surfaces in 
contact with the animals that are not impervious to moisture can absorb moisture and harbor bacteria while being unable 
to be properly cleaned and sanitized possibly exposing the animals to disease hazards. Correct by ensuring all walls,

boxes, houses, dens, and other surfaces in contact with the animals in sheltered housing facilities are impervious to 
moisture. 
 
This is a 2nd Re-License Inspection for a Class “A” license. The inspection and exit briefing were conducted with the 
applicant. 
 
All items must be in compliance within 1 more inspection or by March 15, 2026 or the applicant will forfeit the license fee 
and must wait 6 months to reapply. Please contact your inspector to schedule your next Re-License Inspection. 
 
Regulated activity can continue under your existing USDA license until it expires.
